title: Annual leave
sub_pages: Directing an employee to take annual leave
content:
Annual leave

Annual leave (also known as holiday pay) allows an employee to be paid while having time off from work.

Who gets annual leave:
All employees EXCEPT casual employees get paid annual leave. This entitlement comes from the NES. Awards, enterprise agreements and other registered agreements can't offer less than the NES but can give more annual leave.

How much annual leave an employee gets:
- Full-time and part-time employees get 4 weeks of annual leave, based on their ordinary hours of work
- Example: Jane works 20 hours per week (part-time). She accumulates 80 hours of annual leave per year (equivalent to 4 weeks for her)

Shiftworkers: Entitled to MORE than 4 weeks if their award or EA includes shiftwork provisions and defines shiftworkers as employees who receive the additional week under the NES.

How annual leave accumulates:
- Accumulates from the FIRST DAY of employment, even during probation
- Accumulates gradually and unused annual leave rolls over year to year
- Accumulates when on: paid annual leave, paid sick/carer's leave, paid family and domestic violence leave, community service leave including jury duty, long service leave
- Does NOT accumulate when on: unpaid annual leave, unpaid sick/carer's leave, unpaid parental leave
- Australian Government's Paid Parental Leave Scheme is NOT considered paid leave for accumulation purposes
- Leave does not accumulate for a period of annual leave that has been cashed out

Source: Fair Work Act 2009 sections 86-87

Payment for annual leave:
Annual leave is paid at the employee's CURRENT BASE PAY RATE for all hours of leave taken. This does NOT include extra payments such as overtime rates, penalties, allowances and bonuses.

Awards, enterprise agreements and other registered agreements can set out:
- a different method of payment for annual leave
- that annual leave loading is paid for annual leave

Annual leave when employment ends:
When employment ends, employers must pay their employee for any UNUSED annual leave accumulated during employment. The annual leave payment must be the same amount the employee would have received if they'd taken the leave during employment. This includes annual leave loading and other payments — these apply even if an award, registered agreement or employment contract says they don't.

Annual leave when sick or injured:
If an employee is sick or injured while on annual leave, the employee can use their paid sick or carer's leave entitlement INSTEAD of using annual leave. An employer CANNOT direct an employee to take annual leave while they're taking sick or carer's leave.

Source: Fair Work Act 2009 sections 16, 89(2), 90
